title: SpaceX scrubs Starship launch after some of its engines didn't start
gist: SpaceX scrubbed Starship Flight 13 (its 13th full-scale test and second flight of the Raptor 3-powered Starship V3) after four of the Super Heavy booster's 33 engines failed to ignite; SpaceX is swapping two Raptor engines with the next attempt likely early the following week, continuing a pattern of engine-startup and reignition issues carried over from Flight 12.

themes.theme: Starship V3 / Raptor 3 reliability track record

insights.claim: SpaceX scrubbed Starship Flight 13 at T-0 after computers detected that some of the Super Heavy booster's 33 Raptor 3 engines failed to ignite during startup; a live-stream graphic indicated four of 33 engines never lit, and SpaceX is replacing two Raptor engines before the next attempt, expected early the following week.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Recurring engine-ignition reliability issues on the newest Raptor 3/Starship V3 hardware are a direct near-term risk to SpaceX's launch cadence targets underpinning its Starlink, orbital data center, and NASA Artemis lunar lander commitments; each scrub/engine-swap cycle adds days-to-weeks of delay.
insights.quote: Some of the engines didn’t start, triggering an automatic launch abort
insights.locator: Elon Musk posted on his social media platform X

insights.claim: This was the second flight of SpaceX's upgraded Starship V3 rocket with Raptor 3 engines; the first flight in May was mostly successful on ascent but experienced in-flight Raptor 3 issues, and some of the booster's 33 engines also failed to reignite during its May landing burn, preventing a controlled splashdown.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: A pattern of engine-reliability problems spanning two consecutive Starship V3/Raptor 3 flights (ignition failures on ascent and failed reignition on landing) suggests the newest engine generation is not yet as mature as the prior Raptor/Starship V2 combination that already achieved booster reuse.
insights.locator: The Starship V3 rocket and Raptor 3 engine debuted

insights.claim: SpaceX modified the Super Heavy booster's post-separation flip-maneuver startup sequence for Flight 13 specifically to fix a Flight 12 issue where slight differences in engine startup timing caused the booster's flip to be off by about 90 degrees.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Shows SpaceX's iterative root-cause fixes are targeted at very specific timing/sequencing failure modes, useful context for judging how quickly SpaceX can resolve reliability issues versus how many distinct failure modes remain (ignition, flip, reignition) before Starship is ready for routine operational flights.
insights.quote: At stage separation on Flight 12, slight differences in engine startup on the ship caused the directional flip of the booster to be off by approximately 90 degrees
insights.locator: SpaceX wrote in a recap of the May launch

insights.claim: A successful Flight 13 would be a prerequisite for SpaceX moving Starship to orbital flight, which is itself needed before Starship can be used for Starlink satellite launches, orbital refueling tests, and NASA's Artemis lunar lander program.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Ties Starship test-flight reliability directly to the critical path for NASA's Artemis program and SpaceX's Starlink/orbital-data-center buildout plans (see companion Ars piece on orbital data center economics); continued scrubs and engine failures are a direct schedule risk to those downstream programs.
insights.locator: A successful test flight with the next Starship
